schools.summary: Identify Antipas the tetrarch, his realm and title, and the surprise he had not heard of Jesus earlier; note the Synoptic parallels.
Harmonise the order against Luke/Mark, reject metempsychosis, distinguish the two Herods, and contrast Gentile readiness to believe.
Christ's fame reached even Herod's court, leaving the Jews without the excuse of ignorance.
Chrysostom: the powerful learn slowly; yet Herod fears John even dead — a witness to virtue.
Origen catalogues the Sadducean and Pharisaic opinions on resurrection behind Herod's fear.
Henry: a guilty conscience torments Herod; conviction is not conversion.

outliers.note: Reads the episode as showing the Gentile heart more disposed to faith than the Jewish.

schools.summary: Herod's Sadducean leanings versus his confession of a risen John; the rival popular opinions (Elijah, a prophet) and the verb 'work in him'.
Calvin: the rumour rose first among the people; Herod believed what he dreaded; the misapplied Elijah promise.
The power of conscience — 'Sadduceeism staggers when conscience awakes'; guilt accuses but cannot save.

debates.question: Was Herod a Sadducee, and if so how could he confess a resurrection?

sides.position: He held Sadducean opinions, overborne by a guilty conscience
The conjecture began among the people and Herod adopted it from fear

schools.summary: Reconstruct the genealogy of Herodias, the adulterous-incestuous marriage forbidden in Leviticus, and locate the prison at Machaerus from Josephus.
Calvin prefers the Gospel's reason (reproof) over Josephus's political one and draws the duty of fearless reproof of princes.
Origen: the killing of the last prophet withdrew the Jews' power of execution, fulfilling Genesis 49:10.

debates.question: Why was John imprisoned — reproof of the marriage, or Herod's political fear (Josephus)?

sides.position: For reproving the unlawful marriage, as the Evangelists say

outliers.note: Typological reading: John's death marks the end of the Jewish rulers' power of execution.

schools.summary: Ground the reproof in the Levitical law against a brother's wife; the repeated imperfect 'said'; Josephus on Herod's political fear.
John's boldness as a manual for ministers — plain, courageous reproof of the great, soft words breaking the force of truth.
Gill: John's zeal and courage show he came in the spirit of Elijah.
The Cyril of Jerusalem excerpt is catechetical (apostles above the prophets), unrelated to this verse.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the apostles' dignity, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: Calvin and Gill reconcile Herod's wish to kill with his reverence: conscience and reverence restrained him, then fear of the people; the rule never to flatter princes' sins.
Herod's struggle of conscience against passion (Mark 6:20), paralleled with Ahab and Elijah; fear of man not God restrains him.
Herod would have killed John in passion but was restrained by fear of the multitude and reverence.

debates.question: How does Herod's wish to kill John square with his reverence for him (Mark 6:20)?

sides.position: Reverence and conscience restrained him at first, then fear of the people

outliers.note: The entry here treats the sign-seeking of Matthew 12, not this verse.

schools.summary: Moralise the birthday and dance (Jerome, Remigius, Gregory, Isidore): only the wicked keep birthdays; an evil oath binds no one; Hilary allegorises the dance as pleasure from unbelief.
Salome's public dancing violated eastern modesty; the Jewish view of birthday-keeping as a Gentile custom; Herodias engineered it.
Calvin and Gill: lawful festal joy profaned by luxury, opening the way to murder (cf. Job's sacrifices).
Chrysostom: a twofold charge against the girl — dancing, and asking an execution as her reward.
Clarke and Wesley: the dance and feast as the occasion of the crime; the daughter infamous thereafter.

outliers.note: Allegorises the dancing daughter as pleasure from unbelief and John's head as the glory of the Law surrendered to it.

schools.summary: The oath as a rash, unlawful promise: Barnes and Lightfoot on its sinfulness and Talmudic form; Ellicott on imperial extravagance; Robertson on the Greek and the Esther parallel.
Gill: Herod was charmed by the dancing and the court's pleasure into the promise.
The request as the custom of proof-of-death; the timing of the mother's prompting (after the dance, per Mark 6:24); the men-only supper; lexical notes on 'charger'.
Moral horror at the mother and daughter; Wesley notes 'Give me here' — fearing Herod would relent if given time.
Gill: the daughter hastened in by a mother eager for revenge.

debates.question: Was the girl told what to ask before she danced or only after?

sides.position: Only after, on going out to consult her mother (Mark 6:24)

schools.summary: The oath was no excuse — a prior law against murder cannot be dissolved; Herod feared to be thought weak before his guests; Barnes turns it against the 'laws of honour' and duelling.
Gill catalogues Herod's several sorrows, including the Roman scruple against executions on a birthday.
Clarke on the plural 'oaths' and the guests' code of honour; Wesley: he murdered from 'tenderness of conscience'.

themes: the binding of an evil oath

schools.summary: Details of the executioner and causative verb; Lightfoot's chronology (Machaerus to Tiberias, near the Passover); Josephus on Herodias's influence; Ellicott's eulogy of John's greatness.
Wesley on the mystery of providence permitting a holy man's death, with confidence in heavenly recompense.
Nearness of the prison; the gruesome scene and rejection of Salome legends (Robertson); the later deposition and banishment of Herod and Herodias from Josephus (Ellicott).
Clarke's warning that a reproved lascivious woman is most revengeful, and ministers most at risk.

outliers.note: The note here concerns Nineveh's repentance, not this verse.

schools.summary: Why the disciples came to Jesus (consolation, warning, His own concern); John's disciples transferring allegiance though a remnant persisted (Apollos, Ephesus).
Origen: the withdrawal teaches lawful flight from persecutors (Matthew 10:23), and figures a turning to the Gentiles.
Clarke's moral warning against feasting and dancing as snares for souls.
Gill: the disciples likely sought leave to take the body, as Joseph of Arimathea for Jesus.
Geography of the crossing (Lightfoot) and the prudent withdrawal to safety; added motives of rest for the Twelve and the nearing Passover (Ellicott).
Jerome: He withdrew in mercy, not fear, leaving an example against rashness; Hilary allegorises the ship as the Church passing to the Gentiles.
Chrysostom: He withdrew to prolong the economy of His humanity, confirming the true incarnation; the crowds follow undeterred.
Calvin reconciles the Synoptics on the reason and notes few of the crowd truly adhered (John 6:66).
Henry: the saints' sufferings further the gospel; withdrawal drew the people the more.

debates.question: Did Jesus withdraw from fear of death or for another reason?

sides.position: Not from fear but prudence/mercy and to set an example
To prolong the economy of His humanity and confirm the incarnation

outliers.note: Mystical: the ship is the Church, the desert the Gentile world, the crowd moving from synagogue to Church.

schools.summary: The compassion verb and its 'sheep without a shepherd' background (Mark 6:34); the scribes' neglect; Christ going forward to meet the crowd.
Calvin: compassion for famishing souls; Christ lays aside His own rest for duty and still pities the shepherdless from heaven; teaching preceded healing.
Origen reads the sicknesses as diseases of the soul (avarice, ambition, lust) that Christ heals, distinguishing sin from sickness.

outliers.note: Allegorises the healings as cures of spiritual diseases of the soul.

schools.summary: Typology of the five loaves as the Law and two fishes as the Prophets, broken to feed the Gentiles; the evening as the age's close or the Lord's death (Jerome, Hilary, Raban, Remigius).
The 'first evening' from three o'clock; a lonely not desolate place; Ellicott on the only four-Gospel miracle and the inadequacy of rationalising it; the two hundred pennyworth.
Chrysostom: the desert clears the miracle of suspicion; the Lord waits to be asked; 'a table in the wilderness' (Psalm 78:19).
Origen: 'the time is past' as the season of Law and prophets passing at the 'last hour'.
Calvin: Christ purposely delayed so the disciples would attend and learn He never ceases to care for His people.
The astounding emphatic command and the testing of Philip (John 6:5); Christ requires obedience and supplies the strength.
Calvin: Christ now cares for bodies too (Matthew 6:33), works through the disciples' hands; be content with barley-bread.
Clarke: begin at Christ's command and the bread of God multiplies in the minister's hands.
Origen allegorises the loaves as Scripture's words and the fishes as the spoken/conceived word.
The disciples quick with reasons for inability; the barley loaves of the lad (John 6:9); Lightfoot's rabbinic discussion of 'salt fish' and blessing.
Clarke: conscious need leads to firmer faith; God brings His servants low to prove His kindness.

outliers.note: The note here concerns Jesus's relatives in Mark 3, not this verse.

schools.summary: Robertson: the contrast of the disciples' helpless doubt and Christ's confident courage; they overlooked His power.
Gill: the command tries their faith, obedience and liberality; bring what we have to Christ's hands for increase.
Clarke: nothing is good without God's blessing; the least, brought by faith, becomes sufficiency and a sacrament.
The orderly reclining and easy counting; 'bless' as thanksgiving with the Jewish grace; bread broken not cut; Robertson defends the nature-miracle and Ellicott sees a eucharistic foreshadowing.
Calvin: food is sanctified by thanksgiving (1 Timothy 4:5); looking up rouses us from sloth; Gill on the orderly distribution.
An undoubted creative miracle attested by thousands; the travellers' baskets (cophinus, Juvenal); 'that nothing be lost' as economy; fragments for the poor.
Calvin: the surplus shows provision for future want and is the same grace as the annual increase of corn, hidden only by our ingratitude.
The number magnifies the miracle; all four Gospels count 'men'; women and children grouped apart and uncounted but fed; the crowd judged Jesus 'that prophet' (John 6:14).
Origen allegorises the counted men, women and children as degrees of spiritual maturity.

outliers.note: Reads the men/women/children allegorically of spiritual worth and maturity.

schools.summary: The strong 'compelled' explained by the king-making crowd (John 6:15); the disciples caught in mob enthusiasm; geography toward Bethsaida; retiring alone to pray under pressure of honour.
The ship as the Church tossed by persecution but unsinkable while Christ prays; the four watches as the ages to His return; defence of His true body against Marcion and Theodore.
Chrysostom: He separates the disciples to verify the loaves-miracle and tosses them all night to train endurance and longing for Him.
Calvin: their submission in obeying against their own judgement is the rule of true obedience.
Henry: they met the storm in the very path of duty — no new thing for Christ's disciples.

debates.question: Why did Jesus 'constrain' the disciples to leave?

sides.position: To remove them from the crowd's attempt to make Him king (John 6:15)
To verify the loaves-miracle in His absence and to train them through the storm

outliers.note: Reads the four watches as the ages of Law, Prophets, Incarnation and the Second Coming, the ship as the persecuted Church.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the wilderness is the 'mother of quiet'; solitude is good for prayer; Christ withdraws to train the disciples through the storm.
Robertson: He needed the Father's sympathy amid enthusiasm and misunderstanding; Lightfoot on 'evening' as night; Clarke's conditions of praying well.
Calvin: as God-man He truly prayed; solitude disposes the heart, yet Paul bids prayer everywhere (1 Timothy 2:8) — no fixed rule.
The ship's distance and the lake's breadth; the verb 'tossed/plunged'; the boat bobbing in the choppy sea (Mark 6:48).
Calvin: the storm was permitted to fix their attention on Christ's help; their fault was forgetting the loaves — a hardened heart needing new eyes (Deuteronomy 29:4).
The Cyril of Jerusalem excerpt is catechetical, on the firmament and the waters, not on this verse.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on creation and the waters above the firmament, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The Roman fourth watch (3-6 a.m.) and the Jewish reckoning (Lightfoot); the walking as a creative miracle (Job 9:8); Ellicott guards against minimising it and sees an anticipation of the spiritual body.
The conditions of the fright (dark, storm, no expectation of Christ); the ancient belief in appearing spirits; 'phantasma' as ghost and sailor superstition.
Clarke defends the belief that the spirits of the dead may appear, as held by holy men.
The Cyril of Jerusalem excerpt is catechetical, on the Holy Spirit, not on this verse.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the Holy Spirit, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The unique power of Christ's voice to give courage; Ellicott on the words preserved by all three Evangelists and become the Church's watchword in distress.
Calvin: confidence is founded on His presence; He first corrects fear lest it destroy faith; the same voice is peace to believers, terror to the reprobate.
Peter's ardour and rashness — less faith than he supposed; Matthew alone records it; yet taking Christ at His word is sound reason (Clarke).
Calvin: the 'if it be thou' shows unsettled faith; a good principle in faulty excess; beware excessive haste and overleaping the Lord's bounds.
Gill: Christ says 'Come' to assure them who He is and to confirm Peter's faith by a further instance of His power.
Clarke: Christ gives power to do the impossible to faith, but we must never test His power for curiosity or display.
Barnes continues his assessment of Peter's character from the previous verse.
Peter sinks from looking at the danger, not from the storm — the failure of faith (Clarke); the conflict of sight and faith (Ellicott); the urgent cry to be saved quickly.
Wesley: though a skilful swimmer, when grace acts the natural strength is withdrawn, that he may depend on Christ.
The immediate rescue and the verb 'doubt' as being pulled two ways; look to Christ's upholding power, not the danger (Clarke, James 1:6).
Calvin: Christ reproves the doubt directly opposed to faith — Peter had an express command and recent proof, yet fell into wicked fear.
Cyril of Jerusalem (here on point): faith's buoyancy upheld Peter on the water, and relaxing faith let him sink.

debates.question: Does every fear betray a weakness of faith?

sides.position: Only the doubt directly opposed to faith, against a clear word, is faulted

schools.summary: The instant calm as fresh proof of His power; the ship at once at land (John 6:21); the wind 'grew weary' before its Master; Ellicott on the disciples' amazement and their hardened hearts toward the loaves.
Gill: the calming is ascribed to Christ's coming aboard, not Peter's.
The confessors as crew/passengers anticipating the disciples' faith (Ellicott, Clarke); Jesus accepts worship; growth toward Matthew 16:16; the anarthrous 'Son of God'.
Calvin: sailors and disciples alike confess Him Son of God, rendering Him the Messiah's honour as the prophets foretold.
Origen: only the disciples, and they say 'of a truth' (firmer than the demoniacs); the crossing figures the passage to the eternal land.

debates.question: Who made the confession 'thou art the Son of God'?

sides.position: Chiefly the crew or passengers, distinct from the disciples

themes: confession of the Son of God

schools.summary: Gennesaret as the rich western plain including Capernaum, toward which they steered (John 6:24); the eager touching of His hem (Matthew 9:20).
Typology on the name: Christ guides the Church from persecution into harbour; the 'hem' as His least command or His assumed body (Jerome, Raban, Remigius, Hilary).
Chrysostom: the people's greater faith now brings all the sick to touch His garment; we receive not the hem only but His body.
Calvin: the land filled with proofs of His glory; healing bodies shadows the healing of souls (Isaiah 53:4).
Henry contrasts the eager Gennesarenes with the Gergesenes who begged Him to depart.

outliers.note: Reads the people meeting Christ as believers saved out of the Law, the hem's virtue as the Spirit's grace through the apostles.

schools.summary: They knew Christ by face and gathered all the diseased; Clarke applies it to spreading the news of Christ; Ellicott links the place to the Capernaum discourse of John 6.
Wesley cross-refers to Mark 6:45.

outliers.note: The note here concerns Christ's use of parables in Mark 4, not this verse.

schools.summary: Touching the hem as the woman of Matthew 9:20 did, in faith and humility; the fringe of Numbers 15:38; the touch implies the faith that is the condition of healing; perfect cures for all who apply.
Clarke marvels at the grace working through the hem and prays for increased faith.
